cards.name: Kettlebell Goblet Squat
cards.cardSummary: Kettlebell Goblet Squat is a kettlebell-loaded strength exercise for front-loaded squat strength, bracing, and repeatable depth. Strength is the primary score; explosiveness is delivery-dependent and never the main intent.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare/Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; use enough rest to preserve strength-quality reps and clean kettlebell control.
cards.description: Perform Kettlebell Goblet Squat as a controlled, progressively loaded kettlebell strength pattern. Load, range, tempo, and rest should support force production rather than fatigue chasing or ballistic work.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Goblet squat strength. Score Strength high. Add a small Explosiveness score only when the coach uses crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not reframe this card as a swing, snatch, jump, plyometric, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set the position first, move the kettlebell with control, and finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: The kettlebell gives the athlete a compact but demanding external resistance problem. Its handle, bell shape, and common rack/offset positions require grip, shoulder organization, trunk stiffness, and force production without needing high-impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is loaded force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve. It may support later explosiveness when coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit, a ballistic swing-style drill, or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by kettlebell load, range of motion, base of support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, grip security, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Kettlebell Goblet Squat trains front-loaded squat strength, bracing, and repeatable depth using kettlebell resistance. The athlete organizes the body first, creates tension through the handle and floor, moves through a range they own, and stops the set before technique breaks.
setup: Choose a kettlebell load the athlete can control for every rep.
Set feet, grip, ribs, and pelvis before beginning the movement.
Brace and breathe so the bell stays close to the body and the rep path stays repeatable.
execution_steps: Lower into the pattern with control while keeping the kettlebell close and posture organized.
Reach the target range only if the athlete can own the feet, knees, hips, and trunk.
Stand or return with strong intent without bouncing, twisting, or chasing speed.
Finish each rep looking ready to repeat the same rep again.
coach_cues: Own the start before the rep starts.
Strong, controlled, repeatable.
Brace, then move the bell.
Foot tripod, knees track, chest/ribs stacked.
Stand through the floor, not around the position.
athlete_cues: Strong and clean beats heavy and sloppy.
Stop before the bell controls you.
Make the last rep look like the first rep.
breathing_cues: Inhale to prepare and brace before the hard part.
Exhale through the effort without losing trunk position.
Do not use long breath holds with youth, beginners, older adults, or pregnancy/postpartum athletes.
common_faults: Chasing load after position quality breaks.
Turning the set into a rushed conditioning station instead of strength work.
Knees collapse inward or feet lose tripod pressure.
Depth is forced by rounding the spine instead of owning hip and ankle range.
quality_gate: Athlete can stop the set before grip or alignment fails.
stop_signs: Sharp pain or joint pinching.
Grip becomes unsafe or bell path becomes uncontrolled.
Spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position cannot be maintained.
Breath-holding, dizziness, or pressure symptoms dominate the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use a very light kettlebell, short sets, simple ranges, and coach-controlled rest. Make success position-based, not load-based.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use 2-3 crisp sets with modest load and enough rest to keep every rep teachable and repeatable.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually when bracing, grip, and joint alignment remain stable across all reps and both sides.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest stance or range, moderate load, and an RPE ceiling that leaves 2-3 clean reps in reserve.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress with heavier bells, slower eccentrics, pauses, longer carries, or more demanding unilateral/offset positions without sacrificing alignment.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable supports, slower transitions, lower bracing pressure, and conservative load. Stop before breath-holding, dizziness, or joint irritation appears.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use symptom-guided pressure management. Avoid breath-holding, coning, pelvic heaviness, dizziness, uncomfortable supine positions, or overhead positions that cannot be controlled.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-based loading adjustment. Scale by body size, training age, prior injury, symptoms, current readiness, and coach-observed control.
doNotUseWhen: The athlete cannot control the kettlebell safely.
Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
Grip or posture fails before the target tissues are trained.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.
mediaReferences: Internal Vortex kettlebell strength demo recommended
Vortex Exercise Card Authoring Guide: Capacity / strength sequencing rules
NSCA-style resistance training exercise technique standards
mediaInternalNotes: Film Kettlebell Goblet Squat from front/side where useful.
Keep Strength as the primary tenet.
Explosiveness score is optional and delivery-dependent; do not program as plyometrics or conditioning.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded pattern without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
Athlete can set the kettlebell down safely.
contraindications: Painful joint loading in the pattern.
Uncontrolled spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position.
No safe space, grip, or support for the variation.

cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is loaded force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve. It may support later explosiveness when coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit, a ballistic swing-style drill, or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by kettlebell load, range of motion, base of support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, grip security, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Kettlebell Sumo Squat trains wide-stance leg strength with hip, adductor, and bracing emphasis using kettlebell resistance. The athlete organizes the body first, creates tension through the handle and floor, moves through a range they own, and stops the set before technique breaks.
setup: Choose a kettlebell load the athlete can control for every rep.
Set feet, grip, ribs, and pelvis before beginning the movement.
Brace and breathe so the bell stays close to the body and the rep path stays repeatable.
execution_steps: Lower into the pattern with control while keeping the kettlebell close and posture organized.
Reach the target range only if the athlete can own the feet, knees, hips, and trunk.
Stand or return with strong intent without bouncing, twisting, or chasing speed.
Finish each rep looking ready to repeat the same rep again.
coach_cues: Own the start before the rep starts.
Strong, controlled, repeatable.
Brace, then move the bell.
Foot tripod, knees track, chest/ribs stacked.
Stand through the floor, not around the position.
athlete_cues: Strong and clean beats heavy and sloppy.
Stop before the bell controls you.
Make the last rep look like the first rep.
breathing_cues: Inhale to prepare and brace before the hard part.
Exhale through the effort without losing trunk position.
Do not use long breath holds with youth, beginners, older adults, or pregnancy/postpartum athletes.
common_faults: Chasing load after position quality breaks.
Turning the set into a rushed conditioning station instead of strength work.
Knees collapse inward or feet lose tripod pressure.
Depth is forced by rounding the spine instead of owning hip and ankle range.
quality_gate: Athlete can stop the set before grip or alignment fails.
stop_signs: Sharp pain or joint pinching.
Grip becomes unsafe or bell path becomes uncontrolled.
Spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position cannot be maintained.
Breath-holding, dizziness, or pressure symptoms dominate the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use a very light kettlebell, short sets, simple ranges, and coach-controlled rest. Make success position-based, not load-based.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use 2-3 crisp sets with modest load and enough rest to keep every rep teachable and repeatable.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually when bracing, grip, and joint alignment remain stable across all reps and both sides.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest stance or range, moderate load, and an RPE ceiling that leaves 2-3 clean reps in reserve.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress with heavier bells, slower eccentrics, pauses, longer carries, or more demanding unilateral/offset positions without sacrificing alignment.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable supports, slower transitions, lower bracing pressure, and conservative load. Stop before breath-holding, dizziness, or joint irritation appears.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use symptom-guided pressure management. Avoid breath-holding, coning, pelvic heaviness, dizziness, uncomfortable supine positions, or overhead positions that cannot be controlled.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-based loading adjustment. Scale by body size, training age, prior injury, symptoms, current readiness, and coach-observed control.
doNotUseWhen: The athlete cannot control the kettlebell safely.
Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
Grip or posture fails before the target tissues are trained.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.

Vortex Exercise Card Authoring Guide: Capacity / strength sequencing rules
NSCA-style resistance training exercise technique standards
mediaInternalNotes: Film Kettlebell Sumo Squat from front/side where useful.
Keep Strength as the primary tenet.
Explosiveness score is optional and delivery-dependent; do not program as plyometrics or conditioning.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded pattern without pain.

Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
Athlete can set the kettlebell down safely.
contraindications: Painful joint loading in the pattern.
Uncontrolled spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position.
No safe space, grip, or support for the variation.

cards.athleteLanguage: Set the position first, move the kettlebell with control, and finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: The kettlebell gives the athlete a compact but demanding external resistance problem. Its handle, bell shape, and common rack/offset positions require grip, shoulder organization, trunk stiffness, and force production without needing high-impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is loaded force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve. It may support later explosiveness when coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit, a ballistic swing-style drill, or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by kettlebell load, range of motion, base of support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, grip security, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Kettlebell Floor Press trains horizontal pressing strength with a floor stop that controls shoulder range using kettlebell resistance. The athlete organizes the body first, creates tension through the handle and floor, moves through a range they own, and stops the set before technique breaks.
setup: Lie on the floor or mat with the kettlebell(s) placed where they can be reached safely.
Set feet, ribs, and pelvis before unracking or rolling the bell into position.
Pack the shoulder and keep the wrist stacked before the first rep.
execution_steps: Lower the kettlebell with control until the target stop point is reached.
Keep wrist, elbow, shoulder, ribs, and pelvis organized at the bottom.
Press with strong intent while keeping the rest of the body quiet and stable.
Finish the rep under control, reset the breath and shoulder, then repeat.
coach_cues: Own the start before the rep starts.
Strong, controlled, repeatable.
Brace, then move the bell.
Stack wrist over elbow; press from a packed shoulder.
Ribs down as the bell moves.
athlete_cues: Strong and clean beats heavy and sloppy.
Stop before the bell controls you.
Make the last rep look like the first rep.
breathing_cues: Inhale to prepare and brace before the hard part.
Exhale through the effort without losing trunk position.
Do not use long breath holds with youth, beginners, older adults, or pregnancy/postpartum athletes.
common_faults: Chasing load after position quality breaks.
Turning the set into a rushed conditioning station instead of strength work.
Pressing with rib flare, neck tension, or wrist collapse.
Losing shoulder position at the bottom or overhead finish.
quality_gate: Athlete can stop the set before grip or alignment fails.
stop_signs: Sharp pain or joint pinching.
Grip becomes unsafe or bell path becomes uncontrolled.
Spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position cannot be maintained.
Breath-holding, dizziness, or pressure symptoms dominate the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use a very light kettlebell, short sets, simple ranges, and coach-controlled rest. Make success position-based, not load-based.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use 2-3 crisp sets with modest load and enough rest to keep every rep teachable and repeatable.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually when bracing, grip, and joint alignment remain stable across all reps and both sides.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest stance or range, moderate load, and an RPE ceiling that leaves 2-3 clean reps in reserve.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress with heavier bells, slower eccentrics, pauses, longer carries, or more demanding unilateral/offset positions without sacrificing alignment.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable supports, slower transitions, lower bracing pressure, and conservative load. Stop before breath-holding, dizziness, or joint irritation appears.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use symptom-guided pressure management. Avoid breath-holding, coning, pelvic heaviness, dizziness, uncomfortable supine positions, or overhead positions that cannot be controlled.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-based loading adjustment. Scale by body size, training age, prior injury, symptoms, current readiness, and coach-observed control.
doNotUseWhen: The athlete cannot control the kettlebell safely.
Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
Grip or posture fails before the target tissues are trained.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.
mediaReferences: Internal Vortex kettlebell strength demo recommended
Vortex Exercise Card Authoring Guide: Capacity / strength sequencing rules
NSCA-style resistance training exercise technique standards
mediaInternalNotes: Film Kettlebell Floor Press from front/side where useful.
Keep Strength as the primary tenet.
Explosiveness score is optional and delivery-dependent; do not program as plyometrics or conditioning.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded pattern without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without dizziness.
Athlete can stop the set before technique deteriorates.
Athlete can set the kettlebell down safely.
contraindications: Painful joint loading in the pattern.
Uncontrolled spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position.
No safe space, grip, or support for the variation.

cards.cardSummary: Kettlebell Bottoms-Up Carry is a kettlebell-loaded strength exercise for grip, shoulder, and trunk strength under an unstable upright bell position. Strength and control are the primary scores; explosiveness should not be treated as the main intent.
cards.bestPlacement: Use in the Capacity phase after Prepare/Access, Movement Intelligence, and any true Output work. Keep it before hard conditioning; use enough rest to preserve strength-quality reps and clean kettlebell control.
cards.description: Perform Kettlebell Bottoms-Up Carry as a controlled, progressively loaded kettlebell strength pattern. Load, range, tempo, and rest should support force production rather than fatigue chasing or ballistic work.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: Bottoms-up carry strength. Score Strength high. Add a small Explosiveness score only when the coach uses crisp concentric intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest; do not reframe this card as a swing, snatch, jump, plyometric, or conditioning station.
cards.athleteLanguage: Set the position first, move the kettlebell with control, and finish every rep like you could repeat it cleanly.
cards.whyItWorks: The kettlebell gives the athlete a compact but demanding external resistance problem. Its handle, bell shape, and common rack/offset positions require grip, shoulder organization, trunk stiffness, and force production without needing high-impact or jump-based explosiveness.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the main adaptation is loaded force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve. It may support later explosiveness when coached with intent, but it should not replace true Output work.
cards.commonMisuse: The most common mistake is turning a strength lift into a rushed fatigue circuit, a ballistic swing-style drill, or over-tagging it as explosiveness simply because the athlete moves hard. Keep load, tempo, rest, and technique aligned with strength first.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale by kettlebell load, range of motion, base of support, tempo, set length, and rest. Progress only when posture, pain-free range, grip security, and repeatable rep quality are stable.

coachingExecution.movement_description: Kettlebell Bottoms-Up Carry trains grip, shoulder, and trunk strength under an unstable upright bell position using kettlebell resistance. The athlete organizes the body first, creates tension through the handle and floor, moves through a range they own, and stops the set before technique breaks.
setup: Choose a clear walking lane with enough space to stop safely.
Pick up the kettlebell(s) with a hinge, pack the shoulders, and stand tall before moving.
Set ribs over pelvis, eyes forward, and grip the handle without shrugging or leaning.
execution_steps: Stand tall with the kettlebell(s) under control before the first step.
Walk with quiet, short-to-moderate steps while keeping ribs stacked and shoulders packed.
Keep the bell path stable and avoid leaning, rushing, or turning the carry into conditioning.
Set the bell down with a hinge only after posture and grip are still controlled.
coach_cues: Own the start before the rep starts.
Strong, controlled, repeatable.
Brace, then move the bell.
Walk tall; bell quiet; ribs over pelvis.
Small strong steps, no lean.
athlete_cues: Strong and clean beats heavy and sloppy.
Stop before the bell controls you.
Make the last rep look like the first rep.
breathing_cues: Inhale to prepare and brace before the hard part.
Exhale through the effort without losing trunk position.
Do not use long breath holds with youth, beginners, older adults, or pregnancy/postpartum athletes.
common_faults: Chasing load after position quality breaks.
Turning the set into a rushed conditioning station instead of strength work.
Leaning away from the load or letting the shoulders ride up.
Walking too fast and losing posture before the target distance.
quality_gate: Athlete can stop the set before grip or alignment fails.
stop_signs: Sharp pain or joint pinching.
Grip becomes unsafe or bell path becomes uncontrolled.
Spine, shoulder, hip, knee, ankle, or wrist position cannot be maintained.
Breath-holding, dizziness, or pressure symptoms dominate the set.

scaling.youth_beginner: Use a very light kettlebell, short sets, simple ranges, and coach-controlled rest. Make success position-based, not load-based.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use 2-3 crisp sets with modest load and enough rest to keep every rep teachable and repeatable.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually when bracing, grip, and joint alignment remain stable across all reps and both sides.
scaling.adult_beginner: Start with the simplest stance or range, moderate load, and an RPE ceiling that leaves 2-3 clean reps in reserve.
scaling.adult_advanced: Progress with heavier bells, slower eccentrics, pauses, longer carries, or more demanding unilateral/offset positions without sacrificing alignment.
scaling.older_adult: Use stable supports, slower transitions, lower bracing pressure, and conservative load. Stop before breath-holding, dizziness, or joint irritation appears.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use symptom-guided pressure management. Avoid breath-holding, coning, pelvic heaviness, dizziness, uncomfortable supine positions, or overhead positions that cannot be controlled.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender-based loading adjustment. Scale by body size, training age, prior injury, symptoms, current readiness, and coach-observed control.
pairsWellBefore: Low-intensity conditioning only if strength intent is complete
doNotUseWhen: The athlete cannot control the kettlebell safely.
Pain appears in the loaded pattern.
Grip or posture fails before the target tissues are trained.
The session needs true max-speed or jump Output and this would create fatigue first.

Set the working shoulder, wrist, ribs, and eyes before the loaded rep begins.
Clear the space around the athlete so the bell can be lowered safely at any point.
execution_steps: Roll to the elbow and hand while keeping the kettlebell stacked over the shoulder.
Bridge or sweep the leg into a stable half-kneeling position without losing the overhead line.
Stand tall under control, then reverse the same path back to the floor.
Pause at each checkpoint long enough to prove the shoulder, trunk, and hip positions are owned.

Finish tall with glutes, not low-back arch.

Ribs down as the bell moves.

Squatting the hinge when the goal is posterior-chain loading.
